text: The surgeon prepares to bypass the duodenal obstruction by performing a duodenal jejunostomy. Abdominal insufflation is achieved using a varus needle inserted into the left upper quadrant, with ports placed in a specific configuration for the procedure.

The surgeon begins the procedure by identifying the ligament of Treitz. The transverse colon is retracted cephalad to expose the area. The peritoneum overlying the visibly dilated duodenum is identified and cleared, located to the right of the superior mesenteric artery.

The surgeon brings the jejunum approximately thirty centimeters from the ligament of Treitz into apposition with the duodenum. Stay sutures using zero Surgidac suture are placed around the intended site of anastomosis, with two sutures around the site and a third distally to ensure a straight course for the stapler.

The surgeon creates an anterotomy on the duodenal side using an ultrasonic scalpel, ensuring intraluminal access with a prestige grasper. An anterotomy is also created on the jejunal side. An endo-GIA stapler with a 2.5 mm white load cartridge is introduced into the anterotomy and fired.

The surgeon encloses the common aneurotomy using a 3-0 polysorb suture, carefully evaluating the integrity of both the suture line and the staple line. The trocars are then removed from the surgical field.
